body_txt: Jumping off the slippery slope is when a morally ambiguous character raises an ethical question by doing morally ambiguous things , but instead of answering those questions, the character closes the debate themselves by going on to do something unquestionably and unforgivably evil . Their initial actions remain pretty much ambiguous, but the character no longer is. This is a condensed form of the Slippery Slope Fallacy — instead of Slowly Slipping Into Evil by gradually becoming more evil, they go straight from "may or may not be moral" at the top of the slope to "unquestionably evil" at the bottom, skipping all of the intermediate shades of gray — thus jumping off the slippery slope . This can happen for a variety of reasons; for one, the character might have been more vile or warped than what their initial morally gray actions suggested, or we were seeing their atrocities through a rose-colored lens to begin with . Another is that they went (or are going through) serious Sanity Slippage or Motive Decay in a short period of time, and them jumping off the slope fully underscores just how far they're gone. It might be a particularly grim consequence of hitting someone's Berserk Button or Rage Breaking Point hard enough, giving them exactly the right provocation to make them throw everything away except their rage. A Well-Intentioned Extremist might realize that there is No Place for Me There and become The Unfettered , willing to pursue their goals at any cost, whilst a misunderstood outcast might fully embrace evil after one too many rejections from the "good guys". Finally, sometimes the character simply hasn't been featured much in the story up until that point, and their reprehensible actions simply serves as The Reveal as to how vile they truly are without taking up more screentime than needed. From a more Doylist perspective , this can serve as a form of Debate and Switch , especially when the writers never really address the question of whether the original ambiguous action was acceptable or not. As such, this trope is sometimes a sign that the creator is biased in favor of the less morally ambiguous answer to a conflict, but realized part-way through that the alternative they are trying to argue against makes more sense than they intended to present it as . Sometimes, the intended villain is a full-on Designated Villain , but by the time the writer realizes this, the story is almost finished, so they throw in a gratuitously evil, nonsensical, and out-of-character deed to justify the character being the villain. This also helps to maintain the status quo by ensuring the main characters never question their own morality too closely, thus keeping the simpler Black-and-White Morality from degrading into Grey-and-Gray Morality . Compare and contrast Slowly Slipping Into Evil , for a more drawn-out and visible process of going from "ambiguous" to "evil", and Bitch in Sheep's Clothing a character who pretends to be unambiguously nice for the sake of manipulating others. Also compare with Bait the Dog , Evil All Along , Evil Is Easy , Face–Heel Turn , Moral Event Horizon , Aesoptinum , and He Who Fights Monsters . In case the characters in question were friends prior to Slope-Slipping, it probably triggers We Used to Be Friends . If the character doing the jumping was never bad to begin with, but is doing so out of frustration from being treated like they are, that's Then Let Me Be Evil . If the slippery slope has been greased with Applied Phlebotinum , that's The Dark Side . noreallife
